raw_case:
Unveiling the anomaly: Scimitar syndrome in a middle-aged female patient
Hypogenetic lung syndrome, commonly known as Scimitar syndrome, is a rare cardiopulmonary anomaly characterized primarily by anomalous pulmonary venous return and lung hypoplasia. While it is most frequently diagnosed in infancy or early childhood, cases in adulthood are exceedingly rare. We present a case of a middle-aged female who was diagnosed with Scimitar syndrome.

Introduction

Hypogenetic lung syndrome, also known as congenital venolobar syndrome or more commonly as Scimitar syndrome, is a rare developmental cardiopulmonary abnormality. This condition was first described independently by Raul Chassinat of France and George Cooper of the UK during autopsies in 1836. However, the term “Scimitar syndrome” was coined by Neill et al. in 1960.The most common features of this syndrome include lung hypoplasia and anomalous pulmonary venous return to the inferior vena cava (IVC), with additional features such as pulmonary artery hypoplasia, bronchial anomalies, and systemic arterial supply to the hypoplastic lung [1]. It almost always occurs on the right side, although a few rare cases have been reported involving the left side or both lungs. The condition is more commonly seen in females and may have occasional familial occurrences.Symptoms vary widely and can present at different times, primarily depending on the size of the shunt and the presence of other cardiac anomalies [2].

Case presentation

A 40-year-old female with a dry cough and exertional dyspnea for 3 months was referred from a rural hospital to our hospital. The patient did not have any history of tuberculosis, smoking, or exposure to chemicals and organic dust. There was no history of regular medication or surgery.On examination, the patient was afebrile, and the vital signs were stable. Chest auscultation revealed reduced breath sounds on the right side, along with wheeze and occasional crackles. Cardiac pulsations were noted on the right side of the chest.A chest radiograph was performed, which revealed volume loss of the right lung, ipsilateral tracheal shift, cardiac dextroposition, and tenting of the right hemidiaphragm (Fig. 1). Based on the findings from the chest radiograph, a provisional diagnosis of right lung collapse was made. The differential diagnoses for the etiology included: (a) post-tubercular sequelae (common in this part of the world), (b) neoplastic processes, and (c) mucus plugging or a forgotten foreign body.Contrast enhanced computed tomography (CECT) of chest was performed for better evaluation which revealed anomalous venous drainage into the infra-diaphragmatic IVC (Figs. 2A and B), hypoplastic right lung (Fig. 3A), dextroposition of heart in small right hemithorax (Fig. 3A), hyparterial bilobed right bronchus (Fig. 3B), hypoplastic right main pulmonary artery (Fig. 4A) and anomalous systemic arterial supply from abdominal aorta to right lower lobe (Fig. 4B).Based on the CECT findings, a diagnosis of Scimitar syndrome was made.The patient was counseled about the condition and referred to a cardiologist for echocardiography and further workup. However, the patient did not visit the cardiology outpatient department and was lost to follow-up.

Discussion

Scimitar syndrome is a rare congenital anomaly characterized by an anomalous pulmonary venous return known as the scimitar vein. This vein appears as a vertical curvilinear opacity in the right mid-lower lung, running along the right heart border inferomedially toward the diaphragm to join the inferior vena cava (IVC). Its shape resembles a short, curved sword with the edge on the convex side, traditionally used by Turks and Arabs. When present on a frontal chest radiograph, this finding is referred to as the “scimitar sign,” a term first coined by Dother et al. in 1949.The anomalous pulmonary vein most commonly drains into the infra-diaphragmatic IVC. Less commonly, it may drain into the suprahepatic portion of the IVC, hepatic veins, portal vein, azygous vein, coronary sinus, or right atrium [3]. When partial anomalous pulmonary venous return drains into a systemic vein or the right atrium, a left-to-right shunt occurs, which is usually asymptomatic unless the shunt ratio is 2:1 or greater [4].Hypoplasia of the lung involves the underdevelopment of 1 or more lobes, most commonly affecting the upper and middle lobes of the right lung. The right bronchus is typically bilobed and hyparterial, resembling the morphology of the left lung, i.e., left isomerism. These lung anomalies are important for distinguishing Scimitar syndrome from simple lung hypoplasia and isolated partial anomalous pulmonary venous return. The hypoplastic lung may receive systemic blood directly from the aorta or celiac axis. These arterial branches generally enter the lung through the inferior pulmonary ligament and represent persistent embryonic aortic postbranchial arches that supplied the lung buds before the development of the main pulmonary artery.Pulmonary artery hypoplasia can be mild, which is common in most cases, or significant to the point of being absent. The degree of pulmonary artery hypoplasia determines the extent of systemic arterial supply to the hypoplastic lung [[5], [6], [7]]. In our case, the right pulmonary artery was hypoplastic, and there was systemic arterial supply to the right lower lung from a branch of the abdominal aorta.About a quarter of affected individuals have an associated sinus venosus type of atrial septal defect. Other cardiac anomalies linked with this syndrome include patent ductus arteriosus, aortic coarctation, tetralogy of Fallot, double-chamber right atrium, hypoplastic left heart, and endocardial cushion defects. These cardiac anomalies are most commonly seen in the infantile form of the syndrome. Noncardiac anomalies that may be associated with Scimitar syndrome include pulmonary sequestration (commonly of the extralobar type), horseshoe lung, ipsilateral diaphragmatic anomalies, genitourinary anomalies, and vertebral anomalies [5,7].Three types of Scimitar syndrome have been described [8]. (a) infantile Type: Patients typically present in infancy with severe symptoms. The majority have cardiac anomalies, most commonly atrial septal defects (ASD) and ventricular septal defects (VSD), along with a large left-to-right shunt resulting from the scimitar vein. These patients have a higher incidence of pulmonary hypertension and heart failure [9,10]. (b) adolescent Type: Symptoms and severity vary depending on the degree of lung hypoplasia. Patients commonly present with wheezing, shortness of breath, fatigue, and failure to thrive. (c) Adult Type: Most individuals in this category are asymptomatic and are diagnosed incidentally when imaging is performed for other reasons. Associated cardiac anomalies are usually absent, and the left-to-right shunt is insignificant.The infantile type of Scimitar syndrome is associated with severe morbidity and mortality, with a decrease in severity as the age of presentation increases. In the adult form, diagnosis may be missed due to milder symptoms and the lack of necessary investigations. The dextroposed cardiac shadow can sometimes obscure the scimitar vein, which is often the only clue to the diagnosis on a frontal chest radiograph, as was the case with our patient. In such instances, contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) of the chest plays a valuable role in confirming the diagnosis and identifying associated anomalies.

Conclusion

Scimitar syndrome is a rare congenital cardiopulmonary abnormality. The symptoms and timing of presentation depend on the degree of cardiac anomalies, with most cases presenting in infancy or early childhood. Diagnosis is often made through frontal chest radiography, which typically visualizes the scimitar vein. If this vein is obscured by a dextroposed heart or other pulmonary pathology, multidetector computed tomography (MDCT) is useful for confirming the diagnosis.

Patient consent

All the images used in this manuscript are anonymized.Consent for the publication of this case report was obtained from the patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 40-year-old female.
- **Chief Complaint:** Dry cough and exertional dyspnea for 3 months.
- **History of Present Illness:** No history of tuberculosis, smoking, or exposure to chemicals and organic dust.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** No history of regular medication or surgeries.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** 
  - Afebrile and stable vital signs.
  - Reduced breath sounds, wheezes, and occasional crackles on the right side.
  - Cardiac pulsations noted on the right side of the chest.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  1. Chest radiograph:
     - Right lung volume loss.
     - Ipsilateral tracheal shift.
     - Cardiac dextroposition.
     - Tenting of the right hemidiaphragm.
  2. CECT of the chest:
     - Anomalous venous drainage into the infra-diaphragmatic IVC.
     - Hypoplastic right lung.
     - Dextroposition of the heart in the small right hemithorax.
     - Hyparterial bilobed right bronchus.
     - Hypoplastic right main pulmonary artery.
     - Anomalous systemic arterial supply from the abdominal aorta to the right lower lobe.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- Based on the radiographical findings, initial differential diagnoses included:
  1. **Post-tubercular sequelae:** Excluded due to the absence of history or other supportive features of tuberculosis.
  2. **Neoplastic processes:** Excluded as imaging did not reveal findings suggestive of malignancy.
  3. **Mucus plugging/foreign body:** Excluded due to the lack of history or imaging evidence.
- CECT findings pointed to specific congenital anomalies and confirmed features consistent with **Scimitar syndrome**, such as anomalous pulmonary venous return and lung hypoplasia, leading to its diagnosis.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Scimitar syndrome was diagnosed based on clinical presentation alongside imaging findings including right lung hypoplasia, an anomalous pulmonary vein draining into the infra-diaphragmatic IVC, systemic arterial supply from the abdominal aorta, and associated anomalies.

checked_rare_disease: Congenital partial pulmonary venous return anomaly

results.content:
### Reasoning:

Step 1: Initial assessment of symptoms and clinical presentation.

The patient is a 40-year-old female presenting with a dry cough and exertional dyspnea for 3 months. There's no history of tuberculosis, smoking, or exposure to chemicals and organic dust, which rules out some common causes of respiratory symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and there are no significant personal or family history findings.

Step 2: Review of physical examination findings.

On physical examination, the patient is afebrile with stable vital signs. There are reduced breath sounds, wheezes, and occasional crackles on the right side. Notably, cardiac pulsations are noted on the right side of the chest, which is unusual since the heart is typically located on the left side.

Step 3: Interpretation of chest radiograph.

The chest radiograph shows right lung volume loss, ipsilateral tracheal shift, cardiac dextroposition, and tenting of the right hemidiaphragm. These findings suggest a structural abnormality on the right side of the thorax affecting both the lung and cardiac positions.

Step 4: Analysis of CECT of the chest findings.

The CECT scan provides more detailed information:

- Anomalous venous drainage into the infra-diaphragmatic IVC.

- Hypoplastic right lung.

- Dextroposition of the heart in the small right hemithorax.

- Hypoarterial bilobed right bronchus.

- Hypoplastic right main pulmonary artery.

- Anomalous systemic arterial supply from the abdominal aorta to the right lower lobe.

These findings indicate congenital anomalies affecting the development of the right lung and associated vascular structures.

Step 5: Considering differential diagnoses.

Given the congenital nature of the anomalies noted, possible diagnoses include congenital pulmonary airway malformations, congenital diaphragmatic hernia, or more specifically, conditions like scimitar syndrome or pulmonary hypoplasia.

Step 6: Focusing on scimitar syndrome.

Scimitar syndrome is a rare congenital anomaly characterized by a partial lung lobulation with anomalous venous return to the inferior vena cava, typically via a vein that appears scimitar-shaped on imaging. Other features include pulmonary hypoplasia, cardiac anomalies, and bronchial anomalies.

Step 7: Comparing patient's findings with scimitar syndrome.

The patient's CECT findings align well with scimitar syndrome:

- Anomalous venous drainage into the infra-diaphragmatic IVC.

- Hypoplastic right lung.

- Dextroposition of the heart.

- Hypoarterial bilobed right bronchus.

- Hypoplastic right main pulmonary artery.

- Anomalous systemic arterial supply from the abdominal aorta to the right lower lobe.

These features are classical for scimitar syndrome.

Step 8: Considering other differential diagnoses.

While scimitar syndrome is a strong candidate, other conditions such as pulmonary sequestration or congenital heart diseases could present with similar findings. However, the combination of pulmonary hypoplasia, anomalous venous drainage, and cardiac dextroposition is most consistent with scimitar syndrome.

Step 9: Clinical presentation in the context of scimitar syndrome.

Patients with scimitar syndrome may present with respiratory symptoms such as cough and dyspnea, especially with exertion, due to the underlying pulmonary hypoplasia and vascular anomalies. The symptoms can vary in severity depending on the extent of lung involvement.

Step 10: Confirming the diagnosis.

Given the clinical presentation, physical examination findings, and imaging characteristics, the diagnosis of scimitar syndrome is highly likely. Further confirmation may require cardiac evaluation or consultation with a geneticist, but based on the available data, scimitar syndrome is the most appropriate diagnosis.

### Answer:

Scimitar Syndrome
